1: 'In that day seven women will grab hold of one man, saying: "We will provide our own food and clothing — just let us bear your name and remove our disgrace"' — the consequence of the military catastrophe narrated in Isaiah 3:25 (men falling by the sword): the extreme gender imbalance of war casualties leaves seven women to one man. In the ancient world, marriage required a husband to provide food and clothing (Exodus 21:10 — these were the wife's legal rights); here the desperate women waive this right, asking only for the man's name (to remove the social shame of unmarried status and childlessness). 'Remove our disgrace' (asof cherpatenu, gather away our reproach) — childlessness was the social shame in ancient Israelite culture (cf. Rachel's cry in Genesis 30:23; Hannah's in 1 Samuel 1:11).
2: 'In that day the Branch of the LORD will be beautiful and glorious, and the produce of the land will be the pride and splendour of those of Israel who have survived' — the abrupt transition from the judgment oracle (Isaiah 3) to the salvation oracle: after the purging, the restored remnant will celebrate YHWH's Branch. 'The Branch of the LORD' (tsemach Yhwh) is the first use of this messianic title in the OT. In later prophets: Jeremiah 23:5 ('I will raise up to David a righteous Branch'); Isaiah 33:15; Zechariah 3:8 ('my servant, the Branch') and Isaiah 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch'). Whether 'Branch' here refers primarily to agricultural produce ('the fruit of the land will be the pride') or to a messianic figure is a classic interpretive question — the text may hold both.
3: 'Everyone who remains in Zion and is left in Jerusalem will be called holy — everyone enrolled among the living in Jerusalem' — the holiness of the remnant: those who survive the purging judgment will be designated 'holy' (qadosh) — set apart for YHWH. 'Enrolled among the living in Jerusalem' (hakkathuv lechayyim birushalayyim, written for life in Jerusalem) introduces the imagery of the divine enrollment register — the Book of Life. Exodus 32:32-33 (Moses asking to be blotted from YHWH's book if Israel cannot be forgiven), Psalms 69:28, Daniel 12:1, and Revelation 21:27 (only those written in the Lamb's book of life enter the New Jerusalem) all develop this image of divine registration as the determinant of eschatological survival.
4: 'This will come about when the Lord has washed away the filth of the women of Zion and rinsed the blood from the midst of Jerusalem by a spirit of judgment and by a spirit of fire' — the purification conditions: washing (rachats) and rinsing (shataph) the filth (tso'ah, excrement/filth) of Zion's women (the proud women of Isaiah 3:16-24) and the blood of Jerusalem. 'By a spirit of judgment and by a spirit of fire' (beruach mishpat uveruach ba'er) — the divine Spirit (ruach) as the agent of both legal verdict (mishpat) and consuming fire (ba'er). The Spirit both judges (determines guilt) and purges (burns away the unclean). This anticipates the Pentecostal Spirit of Acts 2 described as fire (Acts 2:3-4).
5: 'Then the LORD will create over every dwelling-place on Mount Zion and over all her gatherings a cloud by day, and smoke with the glow of flaming fire by night; for a covering canopy will rest over all the glory' — the return of the Exodus theophanic cloud: the pillar of cloud by day and fire by night that accompanied Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 13:21-22; Isaiah 40:34-38: the cloud filling the tabernacle when YHWH took up residence). Now YHWH will recreate this presence-sign over all of Zion — not just the tabernacle/temple but 'every dwelling-place' (kol-makhon). The 'covering canopy' (chuppah, wedding canopy/marriage canopy) suggests the eschatological renewal of the YHWH-Israel marriage covenant, with the divine presence as the bridal covering over the whole community.
6: 'There will be a shelter for shade from the heat of the day, a refuge and hiding place from storm and rain' — the practical blessing of the divine covering. The four threats: heat of day (chorev, the scorching summer heat), storm (zerem, violent rainstorm), and rain (matar) — the full range of Mediterranean climate dangers. YHWH as shelter and shade (tsil, shadow) from the sun is a recurring Psalms metaphor (Psalms 91:1: 'under the shadow of the Almighty'; Isaiah 121:5: 'the LORD watches over you — the LORD is your shade at your right hand'). The chapter moves from the stripping of luxury items (Isaiah 3:18-24) to the gift of YHWH's own covering (Isaiah 4:5-6) — replacing human ornament with divine shelter.
